Seeing the Invisible

From Plain Light
to Living Color

A hands-on introduction to brightfield, polarized, and polychromatic polarization microscopy.

Scroll to explore - try the sliders ↓
The puzzle

Two samples. One microscope. They look the same.

Doctors examine tissue under a microscope every day to look for disease.

Stains like H&E do show collagen - but only as flat, monochrome pink. You can see that it's there, not which way the fibres run.

And that direction matters. How do we make it visible?

Step 1 - the everyday microscope

Brightfield: shine light through, add some dye

How it works

White light passes through a thin slice of sample. Whatever absorbs light looks dark.

Why we add stains

Most cells are see-through. Dyes like H&E paint different parts different colours so we can tell them apart.

What it misses

Collagen does show up - but as flat, monochrome pink. You can tell it's there, not which way the fibres run.

A quick idea about light

Light is a wave - and it wiggles in every direction

Think of light as a wave on a rope. Normal light wiggles every which way at once. A polariser is like a picket fence: it only lets through waves wiggling in one direction.

Interactive - crossed filters & Malus's law

Filter 1 polarises the light vertically. Start with Filter 2 crossed (horizontal) and the view is dark. Now flip the sample on: an ordered (birefringent) sample twists the light to a fixed new angle, so some sneaks through even when the filters are crossed. Turning Filter 2 changes how much gets through - but it never changes the light coming out of the sample.
Light getting through
0%
Step 2 - crossed filters

Polarized microscopy: make the hidden glow

Cross two polarisers (90° apart) and the view goes black. Slide a sample in between: most things stay dark, but ordered materials twist the light just enough to sneak past the second filter - so they glow against the black. Collagen is one of them.

birefringence (noun)

When a material is built from lined-up fibres or crystals, light passing one way travels differently than light passing another - so the wave gets twisted. Plain language: messy random stuff stays dark; neatly organised stuff lights up. Polarised light is a detector for order.

But there's a limit

Plain polarization is basically black & white

Plain polarised light tells you where the ordered material is - but mostly as brightness. It struggles to tell you which direction the fibres run. Drag the slider below: left is what plain polarization gives you, right is the upgrade...

Plain polarization PPM
Step 3 - the upgrade

Polychromatic Polarization Microscopy (PPM)

"Polychromatic" just means many colours.

PPM sends in the full rainbow at once. Each colour of light gets twisted by a slightly different amount, so each fibre direction comes out as its own colour - beautiful and packed with information.

Interactive - the colour IS the direction

Drag the slider to turn the fibres and watch the colour change, just like in a real PPM image. Notice horizontal fibres are red whether they point left or right - direction only runs over 180°.
this direction's colour

We can even prove it

Put a target with lines at every known angle under the PPM. Plot the colour it assigns against the true angle and you get a straight line (R²≈1). That calibration lets the microscope turn any pixel's colour straight back into an angle - turning a pretty picture into real measurements.

Under the hood

How PPM turns an angle into a colour

White light is polarised, then a Z-cut quartz crystal rotates each wavelength's polarization by a different amount - so colour gets fanned out across angles. After the sample a second quartz crystal and an analyser only pass light lined up with the analyser. With no collagen the crossed system blocks everything and the field is dark. Collagen twists the light, and the one colour that now lines up gets through - so the fibre's angle picks the colour.

Interactive - set the collagen angle, see the colour

The quartz crystal fans the spectrum into fixed orientations before the sample - the collagen can't touch that. Only once light reaches the collagen does its angle matter: it rotates the angle of the one colour that matches. After the second quartz the other colours are vertical again, so the crossed analyser blocks them - only the tilted colour passes to the sensor. Turn collagen off and everything stays vertical and the field goes dark.
colour that passes
Why scientists are excited

Cheaper, faster, and you can see it with your own eyes

Affordable

Other ways to image collagen need expensive lasers. PPM works on a regular microscope.

Fast

It captures the whole colourful view at once - no slow point-by-point scanning.

See it live

You can view it through normal eyepieces with your own eyes, not just on a screen.

Useful for medicine

Around a tumour, the way collagen lines up can signal whether cancer is spreading - PPM maps that orientation at a glance.

Now it's your turn

Take a look through the PPM

As you look, ask yourself: Where are the fibres? Which way do they run? What story is the colour telling?

Educational use only. Microscopy images and PPM concept based on the work of Michael Shribak (Marine Biological Laboratory).
